featured art

hi! first and foremost, this guide is mainly aimed at my friend group as i hopelessly convince them with big wet sad eyes to make a funny little website . so if you're not from my Years Old Tight Knit Close Friend Group then this guide is probably not very helpful... you can read it anyway if you want tho...

how (and why) you should make a website!

19 Feb 2026

my desperate plea to my dearest friends

hi! first and foremost, this guide is mainly aimed at my friend group as i hopelessly convince them with big wet sad eyes to make a funny little website . so if you’re not from my Years Old Tight Knit Close Friend Group then this guide is probably not very helpful… you can read it anyway if you want tho…


one more note, anything like “i will help you” or “just message me” is directed to my friend group! not to say that if you shoot an email i’ll ignore it (i am happy to answer any questions!!!!!!!!), but again, this is directed To my Friends. internet stranger i may seem nice but i dont know you </3

(on WHY i made this a public blog post, i think the best way to convince my friends 'hey try out my favourite hobby with me!!' is to actually. do the hobby. to show them how cool it is. it's also just fun)


ok em why should i make a website.

there are many benefits to making a website
so i shall list them for you

  1. it’s a way to store / share anything you’d like in a place that isn’t controlled or maintained by someone (or a company) that you have no stakes in
    • for example, imagine you’re using toyhou.se to keep track of all your characters! it’s easy, convenient, and has a long standing history! but what if one day it just. doesn’t exist anymore. if you had a website, it’s another form of a backup!
    • what’s also fun is if you get really good at website-ing, you can eventually make custom pages for your own ocs!
  2. it’s a way to creatively express yourself
    • really kind of a re-hash of the first point, but if you look through the indie web even just for a few minutes, you’ll see all kinds of websites and people and how they express themselves through a visual medium. it’s kind of beautiful, even if that’s really sappy to say
  3. coding (or just HTML + CSS) is an extremely useful skill to have
    • not even just from a “lol job” standpoint, but like. you know how sometimes you go onto a news website and they have an annoying popup demanding your money? you can just open inspect element and delete it. you can do that without any html knowledge, but having that knowledge makes it easier
  4. it’s sick as fuck
    • you gain so much aura from having a website
  5. we could add each other’s buttons and stuff and it would be really cute…


im not super convinced but lets say i make a website. isn’t that really hard

the first time you step into website making it seems so extremely scary and daunting, but it doesn’t have to be! i’m going to break it down to the most simplest of steps!!

(me breaking it down)
credit

step 1. your text editor

to be honest, it really doesn’t matter what your text editor is! some people use stuff like notepad … but if you want my suggestion, i would say use VSCodium! it’s biggest atvantage compared to VS Code, is it being free of microsoft’s grubby little telemetry hands. and that’s enough for me!

step 2. what are you doing

by this i mean: there are two main ways of making a site, at least for beginners. you can either go the route of pure html, or, you can use a static site generator(SSG)! both require you to code and write html. i’ll quickly list the positives and negatives of using either…

pure html static site generator
extremely easy to pick up and learn, you need to learn how to write in basic html for a static site generator, it feels very cozy and nostalgic to write in just html + css very clean and organised, and very easy to update changes across an entire site (say if you use the same template on every page - with regular html, you’d need to manually copy and paste it to every page to update, but with a SSG, you can just update a template file), makes you feel like a coding god
your html can get messy pretty quick, which can make it daunting to update. very annoying to update something across your whole site (say, if you add a new page and want it to be on your navigation, you’ll either need to use javascript, or go back and manually update it on every page you want it on) much higher learning curve, can be slightly annoying to setup on windows compared to linux, can be annoying to upload your site to website hosts


there is no wrong choice - some people prefer to just use raw html and no fancy stuff, but some people like the convenience of static site generators! personally, i love my static site generator life, but i know others can’t stand it.
if i had to steer you towards one or the other, i’d say that unless the idea of using a static site generator interests you, to just go the route of plain html!

step 3. LET THEM COOK

ok now the fun and hard part! actually making the damn website. for the sake of this explanation, i will assume you are using plain html and not going with a SSG.

(if you did want to use a SSG, you should still read the rest of the section, but after, i suggest looking through the jekyll step-by-step tutorial. jekyll is imo the easiest SSG for beginners, and it’s the one i use)

one more quick thing, if you think my explanation is doo doo garbage, you should look at w3schools’ html tutorial instead! it’s made by professionals instead of your dearest silliest friend

to begin, make a folder on your computer! you can name it anything you’d like, but for organisation sake, i tend to just name it the same as the website (meowpricot.art) or as something easily recognisable (my personal site).

you then want to make a file called index.html. this is the webpage that loads when you go to your site! think of the homepage of youtube, or even the homepage of this very website!
open index.html in your text editor of choice! then, you want to copy-paste this into index.html! 

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<title>title of your website</title>
</head>
<body>

<p>hello world!</p>

</body>
</html>


you now have a valid webpage!… but how do you view it?
there is a million ways you can do this, but my two suggestions are:

  1. search “online html editor” and copy and paste your index.html’s contents in the field
  2. install an extension on your text editor. this is what i do for plain html - on VS Code (or codium), go to the extensions panel, and search for something called “Live Server”. install it, and then click on “Go Live” on the bottom right corner - it’ll open a webpage in your browser that will live reload when you make changes!
  3. pick a god and pray that your site works until you upload it online

and there you go! if done correctly, your webpage should just read “hello world” and the tab title should be “title of your website”.

step 4. what does any of this mean???

i will go over everything in that index.html file now.

<!DOCTYPE html>
this is required to be at the start of any .html document. it tells the browser what to expect from your file!

<html lang="en">
the html tag is the root of your html document! it contains all other elements, except for the !DOCTYPE tag. you may also notice the lang=”en” attribute within the html tag! this tells the browser what language your page is in. while it’s not required, you should always include a language attribute!

<head>
the head tag represents metadata for your document. it’s not displayed on your page. it typically defines stuff like your title (as you’ll see next), stylesheets, meta tags, script tags, and more!

<title>title of your website</title>
the title of your site! this appears as the tab title on your browser! you can really make it say anything you want, but try to keep it within 50-60 characters!

<body>
all the stuff that you can see on your website goes in the body tag! for this example, we only have a paragraph…

<p>
a paragraph tag! you don’t actually need to declare paragraphs, you can just plainly write in your body tag. but it’s more Neat and Professional.

you may notice that html, head, title, body and p all have duplicates that have a backslash, like how you can see in </title>. this is how you close a tag - it tells the browser that ‘ok, this element is done now!’ almost all tags need to be closed, and it’s the most common bug you’ll find when making your site. if something is broken, check you closed your tag!

step 5. where do i go now

from here, the sky is the limit baby…
i would suggest you read these pages for further learning! this blog post is getting far too long, and i’m sure you are all asleep at the wheel now
w3schools - html styles & CSS
w3schools - body tag (has a BUNCH of examples for your body section)
w3schools - div tag (divs make the site go ‘round)

or just search up whatever! like “how do i make a div container html” or “how to add a custom font css” or “how to add a background image css”, or you could… always message me…

you can also go browsing the indie web until you find something you like - you can then use inspect element to see how they did it!
IMPORTANTLY, make sure you’re only taking tiny bits and pieces, stealing their entire ass website is rude and it makes you look lazy

step 6. HOW DO I PUT IT ON THE INTERNET

once your site is nice and pretty - or at least digestible, it’s time to make it live for the world to see! you need to pick a website host!
the majority of indie websites are on neocities and nekoweb. you’ll also find them on github pages. there’s also a bunch of smaller hosts, like teacake or leprd.

personally, i’m hosted using pages.gay! it has the benefits that github pages has, without the uncomfy microsoft funding and AI being shovelled down your throat. it also lacks the pseudo-social media aspects that neocities and nekoweb has… and the main thing, i can use my domain name for free…

but honestly, pick the host that speaks to you! there is no wrong choice, and you can always upload your site elsewhere! i would say neocities and nekoweb are the easiest to upload to and use.

you just need to upload your site! neocities and nekoweb both come with a default index.html file that you can replace, or just edit on there directly.

once you’ve uploaded your site… PLEASE SEND IT TO ME I WANT TO SEE IT PLEAAASEEE. I WANT TO BE WEB FRIENDS AND I WANT TO SEE YOUR SITE PLEASE

the end...

that’s it! of course, there’s so much more that you can do with your site, and this is really just the most basic of instructions… honestly, if any of you are actually interested in making a site, i will sit down with you and help you the entire way. but i guess this is more like… ‘a what to expect’! it was also just fun to make, and i can point any of my acquaintances to this page as a “GO READ THIS”

anyway, until next time!!

- with love, meowpricot
